title: Dog Hives Guide: Understanding Red Raised Bumps and Emergency Care
meta_desc: Learn about red raised hives on dogs, why they are high-priority emergencies, and how photos help veterinarians triage allergic reactions quickly.
content_html:
What are these red raised hives?

Red, raised bumps on a dog's body are commonly known as hives, or urticaria. These are often the result of an acute allergic reaction triggered by something your dog touched, inhaled, or ingested. Common culprits include insect stings, vaccines, new medications, or environmental allergens like pollen or certain shampoos.

Is this a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for sudden, body-wide hives is High. While some cases of hives are mild, they can quickly escalate into anaphylaxis, a life-threatening allergic reaction. If you notice hives accompanied by swelling of the muzzle or eyes, heavy panting, vomiting, or collapse, you must transport your dog to an emergency veterinary clinic immediately.

How a photo helps with triage

Capturing a clear photo of the hives is a vital step in the triage process. Skin reactions can change rapidly or even subside by the time you reach the clinic. A high-quality photo allows the veterinary team to see the initial severity and distribution of the bumps, which helps them prepare the appropriate medications and prioritize your pet based on the visual evidence of their physical reaction.

faqs.question: Can hives go away on their own?
faqs.answer: While some mild hives may resolve once the allergen is removed, the risk of respiratory distress or anaphylaxis is high enough that professional veterinary advice is always recommended.

faqs.question: What should I tell the vet when I call?
faqs.answer: Tell them when the hives started, any new foods or environments your dog was exposed to, and if you see any swelling around the face or neck.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog over-the-counter antihistamines?
faqs.answer: Never give human medications to your dog without a specific dosage and approval from your veterinarian, as some brands contain ingredients that are toxic to pets.
